jokes.text: Do you like Little League? Yes. Do you like soccer? Yes. You like jail? No!
jokes.setup: Louie asks Terry innocent questions about activities he likes
jokes.punchline: The third question escalates to asking if he likes jail
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie intimidation tactic with perfect escalation from innocent to threatening. The rhythm of three questions with the absurd final threat is textbook comedy structure.

jokes.text: Terry, uh, what's your last name? I forget. No question he's a match for Banta.
jokes.setup: Terry claims to forget his last name when asked
jokes.punchline: Louie sarcastically comments that Terry is as dumb as Banta
jokes.explanation: Good character-based humor that establishes both Terry's stubbornness and Louie's cutting wit, plus reinforces Banta's reputation for being dim.

jokes.text: I called the cops. The police. Thumbscrews. Batons.
jokes.setup: Louie reveals he called the police on a 10-year-old
jokes.punchline: He lists increasingly medieval torture methods
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's over-the-top vindictiveness toward a child over $13. The anachronistic torture references add absurdist flavor.

jokes.text: Just be the card. Be the hat. Don't question! Be.
jokes.setup: Jim is apparently teaching someone about cards or magic
jokes.punchline: His advice becomes increasingly mystical and nonsensical
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim pseudo-philosophical rambling. The escalation from practical advice to zen nonsense is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Does that work for everything? I don't know. It always worked for me for parallel parking.
jokes.setup: Someone asks if Jim's mystical 'be the thing' advice works universally
jokes.punchline: Jim matter-of-factly says he uses it for parallel parking
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jim moment - takes his mystical nonsense and applies it to the most mundane driving task. The deadpan delivery sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: I might never have turned out to be the man I am today.
jokes.setup: Jim reflects on how being brought home early from running away might have changed his life
jokes.punchline: The implication that his current state is somehow a positive outcome
jokes.explanation: Jim's complete lack of self-awareness about his obvious dysfunction creates perfect ironic comedy.

jokes.text: Yeah, you see that, kid? Tell us your name before your brain sprouts fungus.
jokes.setup: Louie uses Jim's philosophical rambling as a cautionary tale
jokes.punchline: Warns Terry his brain will 'sprout fungus' if he doesn't talk
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie line - uses Jim's obvious mental issues as a threat while delivering a memorably grotesque image.

jokes.text: We'll take care of him, Louie. He belongs to our species.
jokes.setup: Someone volunteers to handle Terry instead of letting Louie intimidate him
jokes.punchline: Refers to decent humans as a separate species from Louie
jokes.explanation: Clever insult that positions Louie as literally inhuman while being protective of the child.

jokes.text: I hate it when they do that! Oh, Elaine.
jokes.setup: Elaine describes how her son gives her the silent treatment when upset
jokes.punchline: She immediately demonstrates her own tendency to overshare inappropriate details
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's tendency to get too emotional about parenting topics, creating an awkward moment.

jokes.text: Where did you say they lived? Nice try, Alex.
jokes.setup: Alex tries to trick Terry into revealing where his parents live
jokes.punchline: Terry immediately sees through the obvious ploy
jokes.explanation: Good reversal where the 10-year-old outsmarts the adult with a tired interrogation technique.

jokes.text: You know, he reminds me more and more of myself. He's not going back, and neither am I.
jokes.setup: Jim identifies with Terry's situation
jokes.punchline: Jim declares he's also not going back (to where?)
jokes.explanation: Jim's mysterious declaration raises questions about what he's supposedly escaping from, adding to his enigmatic character.

jokes.text: You prod me one more time, I'm gonna show you eight places you can beat a man and leave no marks.
jokes.setup: Louie is apparently prodding or annoying a police officer
jokes.punchline: Officer threatens very specific and professional-sounding police brutality
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that plays on police brutality in a way that was edgier for 1980s television. The specificity of 'eight places' makes it memorable.

jokes.text: So what? So you're big, so you're black, so you got a badge, so you got a gun. I'm a dead man.
jokes.setup: Louie lists all the reasons he should be intimidated by the officer
jokes.punchline: Concludes with fatalistic acceptance that he's doomed
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's inability to back down even when completely outmatched. His self-destructive stubbornness is both funny and in-character.

jokes.text: The one I established a liking for so quickly?
jokes.setup: Terry identifies himself as 'the kid from the garage'
jokes.punchline: Jim's overly formal and immediate way of describing their brief acquaintance
jokes.explanation: Jim's peculiar, formal way of acknowledging relationships shows his emotional distance despite apparent warmth.

jokes.text: Well, they said, 'Go sit in that chair, kid.' So I did. Then after a while, I got up and walked out. Was anybody hurt?
jokes.setup: Jim asks how Terry escaped police custody
jokes.punchline: Terry describes casually walking out, Jim's only concern is potential casualties
jokes.explanation: Great two-part joke: Terry's matter-of-fact escape from police, then Jim's completely inappropriate follow-up question treating it like an action movie.

jokes.text: You're the only Jim Ignatowski in the phone book. I know. Isn't it great?
jokes.setup: Terry explains how he found Jim's address
jokes.punchline: Jim is delighted by his unique name rather than concerned about privacy
jokes.explanation: Jim's childlike pride in having a unique name shows his lack of normal adult concerns about safety or privacy.

jokes.text: What do you see? Ice cream, popcorn and television. Exactly. What kind of place is this for a kid?
jokes.setup: Jim asks Terry to look around his apartment, implying it's inappropriate for children
jokes.punchline: Terry lists things any kid would love, but Jim treats this as proof it's unsuitable
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal where Jim's attempt to be responsible completely backfires. His backwards logic about child-rearing is both absurd and in-character.

jokes.text: I don't even have Atari.
jokes.setup: Jim continues listing reasons his place isn't suitable for a child
jokes.punchline: His biggest concern is lacking the era's premier video game system
jokes.explanation: Good period detail that shows Jim's skewed priorities - he's more concerned about entertainment than actual child welfare.

jokes.text: I can't ride my bike in any streets where there's traffic. Oh, no!
jokes.setup: Terry lists his parents' 'terrible' restrictions
jokes.punchline: Jim is horrified by completely reasonable safety rules
jokes.explanation: Jim's reaction to basic child safety as if it were abuse perfectly captures his complete disconnect from normal parenting.

jokes.text: Terry! I'll never send you back to a hellhole like that!
jokes.setup: Terry finishes describing his parents' basic rules and homework requirements
jokes.punchline: Jim dramatically declares the normal household a 'hellhole'
jokes.explanation: Perfect climax to the setup showing Jim's complete inability to distinguish reasonable parenting from abuse. The dramatic overreaction is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: What have you got? Well, sir, we got... uh... chili! Terry... SpaghettiOs.
jokes.setup: Terry asks what's for breakfast
jokes.punchline: Jim offers chili, then settles on SpaghettiOs as the breakfast option
jokes.explanation: Shows Jim's complete inability to provide proper meals, with the formal 'sir' adding to the absurdity of the terrible food options.

jokes.text: Good for you. After all, breakfast is the most important meal of the day.
jokes.setup: Terry admits to eating only junk food for breakfast
jokes.punchline: Jim praises this while reciting nutritional wisdom
jokes.explanation: Jim's ability to rationalize terrible parenting decisions with conventional wisdom creates good ironic humor.

jokes.text: Let's play catch. In the house? Yeah. Wow! My parents would never let me play catch in the house. Mine, neither.
jokes.setup: Jim suggests playing catch indoors, Terry notes his parents wouldn't allow it
jokes.punchline: Jim agrees his parents wouldn't either, but does it anyway
jokes.explanation: Jim acknowledges normal parenting wisdom while immediately ignoring it, showing his rebellious-child mentality despite being an adult.

jokes.text: Well... Parents know a few things.
jokes.setup: They've clearly just broken something while playing catch indoors
jokes.punchline: Jim's understated admission that parents might have valid reasons for rules
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the indoor catch setup. Jim's deadpan acknowledgment after obvious consequences is expertly timed and delivered.

jokes.text: Well, that's the nice thing about catch. It doesn't have to be long, just so it's good.
jokes.setup: Their game of catch has ended abruptly (presumably from breaking something)
jokes.punchline: Jim philosophically rationalizes the short duration
jokes.explanation: Jim's ability to turn disaster into zen wisdom is perfectly in character. The line also works as unintentional innuendo, adding another layer.

jokes.text: If I had dropped out of grammar school, I'd never have been able to drop out of college.
jokes.setup: Jim explains the importance of staying in school
jokes.punchline: His logic is that education was necessary to enable him to drop out later
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jim logic that perfectly captures his backwards thinking. The line has become a classic example of his character's twisted wisdom.

jokes.text: We've all got a little Obi-Wan Kenobi in us.
jokes.setup: Jim agrees to teach Terry what he knows
jokes.punchline: Jim comparing himself to a wise Jedi master
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that shows Jim's delusions of wisdom. The Star Wars reference was still relatively fresh in 1982.

jokes.text: Of course there is. Don't let anybody fool you. People try to make that complicated. If a tree falls, there's a sound! The bigger the tree, the bigger the sound.
jokes.setup: Jim poses the classic philosophical question about a tree falling in the forest
jokes.punchline: He completely misses the philosophical point and gives a literal, practical answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment where he takes a deep philosophical question and reduces it to the most basic misunderstanding. His confidence in the wrong answer makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It was The Kinks. She could have worn the blue thing.
jokes.setup: Jim reveals the moral dilemma was about missing The Kinks concert for his mother's dress
jokes.punchline: Jim's casual dismissal of his mother's needs for his own desires
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reveals Jim failed his own moral test. The casual way he dismisses his mother's anniversary dress shows his selfishness.

jokes.text: Tomorrow... we do history. The '60s, a misunderstood decade.
jokes.setup: Jim outlines their future curriculum
jokes.punchline: His choice of the '60s as the historical focus, implying personal experience
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that hints at Jim's hippie past and his belief that he has special insight into that era.

jokes.text: If you don't mind, will you get it out and re-grout the shower? I'll try. That's all anyone can ask.
jokes.setup: Jim asks Terry to do a complex home improvement task
jokes.punchline: Treats asking a 10-year-old to re-grout as perfectly reasonable
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Jim's complete disconnect from age-appropriate expectations. The casual request for skilled labor from a child is absurdly presented as normal.

jokes.text: Finally I gave in and you know, it wasn't as bad as I thought. I might just start doing it regularly.
jokes.setup: Jim describes a conflict with 'his kid' about washing behind ears
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was talking about washing his own ears, not supervising a child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection where Jim's parenting story is revealed to be about his own basic hygiene. The gradual realization creates perfect cringe comedy.

jokes.text: I got him a library card.
jokes.setup: Jim claims he made the adoption legal
jokes.punchline: His proof of legal adoption is a library card
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim logic - completely misunderstanding legal processes while delivering the punchline with total confidence. The absurdity and economy make this a standout.

jokes.text: It's our duty to sound them out about a reward.
jokes.setup: Discussion about worried parents desperately searching for their child
jokes.punchline: Louie's only concern is potential financial gain
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - taking a heartbreaking situation and immediately focusing on money. His shameless greed is in character.

jokes.text: Forcing him to take piano lessons, sending him off to camp every summer as if he was a-a camper.
jokes.setup: Jim describes the 'terrible' things Terry's parents do to him
jokes.punchline: Treats summer camp as abuse, then adds 'as if he was a camper' as if that makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Jim's bizarre logic where sending a child to camp 'as if he was a camper' is presented as additional evidence of abuse. The circular reasoning is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: A little spotted puppy who's just sitting around now pining away wondering where his Terry is, tears welling up in his little brown eyes.
jokes.setup: Alex mentions Terry has a puppy to guilt Jim
jokes.punchline: Increasingly elaborate and manipulative description of the sad puppy
jokes.explanation: Good emotional manipulation played for laughs. Alex laying it on thick with puppy imagery shows how desperate they are to get through to Jim.

jokes.text: This is emotional blackmail. The puppy's name is Snowball.
jokes.setup: Jim correctly identifies Alex's manipulation tactic
jokes.punchline: Alex immediately continues with more puppy details
jokes.explanation: Good escalation where Jim's accurate assessment doesn't stop Alex from continuing the manipulation. The timing and persistence create the humor.

jokes.text: It's just not gonna be easy explaining to a ten-year-old boy that he's not adopted.
jokes.setup: Jim reluctantly agrees to send Terry back to his parents
jokes.punchline: Jim's backwards concern about 'un-adopting' someone who was never adopted
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim logic - completely inverting the actual situation. His genuine concern about explaining a non-adoption shows his complete disconnect from reality.

jokes.text: You had 44 cans of it.
jokes.setup: Terry claims he knew SpaghettiOs were Jim's favorite
jokes.punchline: The absurd quantity of canned pasta reveals Jim's terrible diet
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag that paints a picture of Jim's cupboard. The specific number makes the poor nutrition choices more absurd.

jokes.text: Why? Is it time to tidy up his room? He'll be here in plenty of time for summer camp. Don't worry.
jokes.setup: Terry's parents ask where their son is
jokes.punchline: Jim sarcastically references the 'terrible' parenting practices he learned about
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Jim still sees the parents as abusive for normal parenting. His sarcasm reveals his continued misunderstanding.

jokes.text: Come on, Terry. We have your puppy in the car. See ya, Jim. Thanks!
jokes.setup: Jim is struggling with having to give Terry back and expects the same from Terry
jokes.punchline: Terry immediately abandons Jim when he hears about the puppy
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal where the child Jim thinks is bonded to him immediately chooses the puppy. The casual 'See ya' deflates Jim's importance completely.

jokes.text: I just don't know how I'm gonna face him when he discovers that they've found him out.
jokes.setup: Terry has just happily run off with his parents
jokes.punchline: Jim still doesn't understand that Terry wanted to go home
jokes.explanation: Jim's complete obliviousness to what just happened extends the comedy. He still thinks Terry was kidnapped rather than reunited.

jokes.text: Grownups make too big a fuss out of good-byes. I used to hate it. 'Say good-bye to your Uncle Harry.' 'Good-bye, Uncle Harry.' 'Good-bye, Jim.'
jokes.setup: Jim explains why he doesn't want to chase after Terry
jokes.punchline: His childhood memory reveals why he has issues with farewells
jokes.explanation: Good character insight that explains Jim's emotional distance. The specific memory adds depth while maintaining the comedy.

jokes.text: You don't have to call... you don't have to write. All I ask is that you remember me.
jokes.setup: Jim asks to say goodbye to Alex since he couldn't say goodbye to Terry
jokes.punchline: Treats a casual workplace goodbye as if Alex is leaving forever
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation where Jim transfers his farewell drama to an everyday situation. The overwrought sentiment for a normal goodbye is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: How can you make a simple thing so heart-wrenching? I feel awful. I can't leave now.
jokes.setup: Jim has delivered an overly emotional goodbye
jokes.punchline: Alex is genuinely moved despite the absurdity and can't bring himself to leave normally
jokes.explanation: Good payoff showing how Jim's emotional manipulation works despite everyone knowing it's ridiculous. Alex's self-awareness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Yeah, what're you havin'? Uh, SpaghettiOs, popcorn and herring.
jokes.setup: Alex asks what Jim is having for lunch
jokes.punchline: Jim offers another bizarre food combination featuring his staple SpaghettiOs
jokes.explanation: Solid callback that maintains the food humor while showing Jim's consistently poor dietary choices.

jokes.text: No matter how many leave the nest, it never gets any easier.
jokes.setup: Jim is alone after everyone has left
jokes.punchline: Treats the departure of a coworker and a one-night houseguest as if he's an empty-nester
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending that shows Jim's tendency to dramatize normal situations. The 'empty nest' metaphor for his minimal relationships is both sad and funny.
